response:
to solve the problem we need to compute the sum of absolute differences for each element in the sorted array while applying a special rule for every third element. for each index i if it is not a multiple of 3 we compute the sum of absolute differences between nums[i] and all other elements. if it is a multiple of 3 we compute the sum of the squares of the differences instead. since the array is sorted we can optimize the calculation using prefix sums to avoid redundant computations.

we can precompute the prefix sums of the array and then for each element calculate the left and right contributions to the sum. for absolute differences the left contribution is nums[i] * i minus the prefix sum up to i and the right contribution is the suffix sum from i to the end minus nums[i] multiplied by the number of elements to the right. for the square of the differences we iterate through all elements and compute the square of the difference between nums[i] and nums[j]. this approach ensures that we handle the constraints efficiently and avoid timeouts even for large input sizes.

***

by leveraging the sorted property of the array we can reduce the time complexity from o(n^2) to o(n) for the absolute difference part. for the square differences part we still need to iterate through all elements for every third index which results in an overall time complexity of o(n^2) in the worst case. however given the constraints of the problem this approach is acceptable as it ensures correctness and adheres to the rules provided. the key is to handle the two different calculations efficiently based on the index and ensure that the final result array is built correctly.
